params.description: Sticker sheet appears showing 3 rows of 4 stickers in a clear rectangular arrangement.

beats.text: Check out this sticker sheet. The stickers aren't bunched up or scattered around. Every sticker has its own spot. Here's what I notice.

elements.description: Sticker sheet showing 3 rows of 4 stickers in a clear rectangular arrangement.
Sticker sheet showing 3 rows of 4 stickers. Rectangle outline visible around the array.

beats.text: The stickers are lined up. They go across, and they go up and down. Everything lines up into a rectangle.

params.description: System briefly outlines the full rectangle shape around the sticker sheet.

elements.description: Sticker sheet showing 3 rows of 4 stickers in rectangular arrangement.

beats.text: When objects are arranged like this, lined up in a rectangle with every spot filled, that's called an {array}.

params.description: 3×4 array of stickers appears. 3 rows, 4 columns. Neutral state, no highlights.

beats.text: Here's what we call the parts you're looking at. Watch.

params.description: Row 1 (top horizontal line of 4 stickers) highlights.

beats.text: See this line going across? That's a {row}. Rows go across, left to right.

params.description: Row 2 highlights, then row 3 highlights sequentially.

beats.text: Row 1, row 2, row 3. Three rows, all going across.

params.description: All row highlights clear. Array returns to neutral state.
Column 1 (left vertical line of 3 stickers) highlights.

beats.text: Now the other direction. See this line going up and down? That's a {column}. Columns go up and down.

params.description: Column 2, column 3, column 4 highlight sequentially.

beats.text: Column 1, column 2, column 3, column 4. Four columns, all up and down.

params.description: All column highlights clear. Array returns to neutral state.

elements.description: 3×4 array of stickers in neutral state. No highlights active.
3×4 array of stickers in neutral state. multiple_choice tool active.

beats.text: Think about the difference: rows go across, columns go up and down. How many rows going across does this array have?
How many rows? Rows go across.

validator.description: Student selected 3, correct answer

beats.text: Yes, 3 rows going across. So this array has 3 rows and 4 columns. An {array} is equal groups organized into rows and columns.

validator.description: Student selected 4, counting columns instead of rows

params.description: All 3 rows highlight sequentially to show the difference from columns.

beats.text: You counted the columns. Rows go across, not up and down. Count the lines going across.

validator.description: Student selected 7, adding rows and columns together

params.description: All 3 rows highlight sequentially.

beats.text: Let's try again. Count each line going across horizontally. There's one on top, one in the middle, one on the bottom.

validator.description: Student selected 2, miscounting the rows

params.description: All 3 rows highlight sequentially.

beats.text: No, here's what helps: just count the horizontal lines, the ones going across. Don't add anything.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: All 3 rows highlight sequentially with emphasis.

beats.text: Let's try together. Rows go across, left to right. I count the top row, that's 1. Then the middle row, that's 2. Then the bottom row, that's 3. So there are 3 rows.
Remember, an {array} is equal groups organized into rows and columns. This array has 3 rows and 4 columns.

params.description: 2×6 dot array appears. 2 rows going across, 6 columns going up and down.

beats.text: Here's a new array, this time with dots. How many rows does this array have? Remember, rows go across.
How many rows? Rows go across.

validator.description: Student answered 2, correct

beats.text: That's right. 2 rows. The lines going across.

validator.description: Student selected 6: counted columns instead of rows

params.description: Both rows highlight to show horizontal lines going across

beats.text: You counted the columns. Rows go across horizontally. How many lines go across?

validator.description: Student selected 8: likely added rows and columns

params.description: Both rows highlight to show horizontal lines

beats.text: That's not it. We're only counting rows right now. Rows go across. Count the horizontal lines.

validator.description: Student selected 12: counted total dots

params.description: Both rows highlight

beats.text: Let's check again. We're counting rows, not all of the dots. Rows are the lines going across horizontally.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System highlights first row, then second row, counting each

beats.text: Let me show you how to find the rows. Rows go across, like reading. Here's the first row across. Here's the second row across. That's 2 rows total.

2×6 dot array. 2 rows going across, 6 columns going up and down.

beats.text: Now how many columns? Columns go up and down.
How many columns? Columns go up and down.

beats.text: Let's check again. We're counting columns, not all of the dots. Columns are the lines going up and down.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

beats.text: Let me walk you through counting the columns. Columns go up and down. First column. Second column. Third, fourth, fifth, sixth. That's 6 columns total. So this array has 2 rows and 6 columns.

beats.text: 3 rows of 5. That's just like saying 3 groups of 5. You already know equal groups means multiplication. So 3 rows of 5 becomes 3 times 5.
Now here's a new array.

System places 2 tile into second slot. Expression shows 4 × 2.

beats.text: Let me show you how this works. First, I count the rows. I see 4 rows, so that's my first number. Then I count the dots in each row. There are 2 dots in every row, so that's my second number. 4 rows of 2 becomes 4 times 2. The words match the expression.

beats.text: Let's break this down step by step. First, I count the rows. There are 2 rows going across so 2 goes into the first slot. Then I count the dots in each row. Each row has 5 dots which goes in the second slot. The array shows 2 rows of 5 so the expression is 2 times 5.

params.description: 2×5 dot array appears on screen. 2 rows, 5 columns of dots.

beats.text: You've been describing arrays by rows, those lines going across. But there's another way to describe them. Let's explore this together.

beats.text: Same array. Different way to describe it, which gives us a different expression. 2 rows of 5 was 2 times 5. 5 columns of 2 is 5 times 2. Both describe this array.

params.description: Both expressions display briefly: 2 × 5 and 5 × 2 with array between them.

beats.text: Here's what matters: your DESCRIPTION determines your EXPRESSION. What you SAY to describe the array becomes the expression you WRITE. Let's try one.

beats.text: Now the equation. What multiplication does this array show?
Build the equation for your array.
You learned to describe arrays by rows or columns, write expressions for both, and find the product. Let's see how you do with this.
